text: I'm just looking at a few verses this afternoon, and I told someone that I would be preaching for about 45 minutes. They said, well, you know, you could make it 30. And I said, well, you know, I only have three verses for my text, but it doesn't mean we're not going to go other places. And anyhow, I do trust it'll be a blessing to you as we continue our study of the book of Genesis. I've thoroughly enjoyed studying the book of Genesis. This is our 27th lesson from the book of Genesis, and it seems like we've been talking about it for a long time of getting started with the series, and now we're going to be concluding chapter number eight this evening. As you, those of you that have been part of the study, as you know, Noah, as we were introduced to him in chapter number six of the book of Genesis, he was a man that walked with God. He had a great testimony. The Bible tells us that he walked with God, and certainly the example of Enoch, of walking with God, the two men that are first mentioned here in the book of Genesis that had that testimony that they walked with God. And the Bible tells us that Noah found grace in the eyes of the Lord in Genesis chapter number six. As we would consider the testimony of this man Noah, we find that during a time of great wickedness and the corruption that had filled the earth, that Noah continued to maintain a proper walk with God. What a great example for us this afternoon as we would consider that even in the midst of the world in which we live, that we can maintain a God-honoring testimony and we can walk with God. We do not have to be polluted or tainted by this world. He has left us here in this world to be lights and to shine in the midst of a crooked and perverse generation. And I'm thankful for the testimony of Noah as we would consider the fact that he walked with God, that he obeyed the Lord, we also must consider the obedience and the faith of Noah. He was a man that believed God. God told him, here's what's going to happen. I'm going to destroy the earth. I'm going to bring rain. I'm going to bring a flood. And he believed what God told him. God told him to build an ark and he moved and he did, he obeyed and he had faith that God was going to do what God said he was going to do. Off to repeat it in chapter six, seven and eight would outline for us, or really six and seven, the obedience of Noah and that he did all that God commanded him. So did he is how Genesis chapter number six concludes. As you'll recall from last Sunday, as we looked at the eighth chapter of the book of Genesis, we find that it's that time in which Noah and his wife and his three sons and their wives are upon the ark. And I made mention at the end of the message last Sunday evening, and we really kind of focused on seeking to learn this valuable lesson of waiting on God to provide direction in our lives. And the direction was received. Noah opens the door to the ark and he appears, he looks and it seems as if the ground was dry, but yet God did not instruct him to get off the ark at that time. I know that I made mention of the fact that many times as we would consider the waiting on the Lord, waiting is something that most of us are not good at. But yet Noah remained on the ark, he and his family and all of those animals until God spoke to him and God said, now's the time to get off. And what an important lesson for us to learn. As we would consider that message and that truth, we then come to our text this afternoon where the Bible tells us in chapter number eight and verses 18 and 19 that they all got off the ark. Noah went forth and his sons and his wife and his sons' wives with him. Every beast, every creeping thing, every fowl and whatsoever creepeth upon the earth after their kinds went forth out of the ark. Now, this is the first time that they get off the ark in a year and 10 days. That's a long time. That's how long they'd been on the ark. If you were to go back in chapter number seven and you would find when they got on the ark and when God shut the door and you see the timeline all the way through chapter seven into chapter number eight, you find that it's a year and 10 days that has passed since they first got on the ark. Now you consider with me this evening or this afternoon what it would be like to get off the ark after you'd been on the ark for a year and 10 days. Now they're going to step off the ark and they're going to take their first steps on an entirely changed earth. We had mentioned last week at the end of the message how the topography and the geography of earth would have changed drastically from prior to being in the earth when they got onto the ark and then all that took place through the flood and all of the destruction and all of the change to the topography and the geography of earth. And we find that they now step out onto this entirely changed earth. I wonder this evening if we can kind of relate to Noah and his family. I wonder what it is that, how we would feel. I wonder how they felt getting off of the ark. I wonder this evening what we would think about doing. I know that as you take trips and you get on things, people say, the first thing I'm going to do when I get off of or I get out of, you've heard people say that, right? The first thing I'm going to do when I get off this airplane, the first thing I'm going to do when I get off this boat, the first thing I'm going to do when I get out of this car.
